title: Guia de Resubmissão ao CRAN — prLogistic v2.0.0
text: Este guia descreve cada passo, na ordem certa, para subir o pacote ao GitHub com pkgdown e em seguida submetê-lo ao CRAN.

title: Guia de Resubmissão ao CRAN — prLogistic v2.0.0
text: Instale os pacotes de desenvolvimento uma vez:
code: install.packages(c("devtools", "roxygen2", "pkgdown", "usethis",                    "testthat", "knitr", "rmarkdown"))

title: Guia de Resubmissão ao CRAN — prLogistic v2.0.0
text: Acesse https://github.com/new Nome repositório: prLogistic Visibilidade: Public Não marque “Add README” (já temos um) Clique em Create repository

title: Guia de Resubmissão ao CRAN — prLogistic v2.0.0
text: Extraia o zip prLogistic_v2.0.0.zip numa pasta local, depois:
code: cd prLogistic/              # pasta raiz do pacote  git init git add . git commit -m "feat: v2.0.0 — continuous covariates, GEE, survey, S3 class" git branch -M main git remote add origin https://github.com/Raydonal/prLogistic.git git push -u origin main

title: Guia de Resubmissão ao CRAN — prLogistic v2.0.0
text: repositório → Settings → Pages Source: Deploy branch Branch: gh-pages → /root Clique em Save O site será publicado automaticamente em https://raydonal.github.io/prLogistic/ após o primeiro push (o GitHub Actions cuida disso).

title: Guia de Resubmissão ao CRAN — prLogistic v2.0.0
text: Na pasta pacote, R: Confirme que NAMESPACE foi atualizado e que os arquivos .Rd foram criados em man/.
code: library(devtools) devtools::document()   # roda roxygen2, regenera NAMESPACE e man/

title: Guia de Resubmissão ao CRAN — prLogistic v2.0.0
text: Verifique se NEWS.md documenta mudanças da v2.0.0. O CRAN lê isso.

title: Guia de Resubmissão ao CRAN — prLogistic v2.0.0
text: Crie este arquivo na raiz pacote (não é enviado ao CRAN, mas é boa prática manter):
code: ## R CMD check results  0 errors | 0 warnings | 1 note  * New submission (package was archived; this is a resubmission)  ## Tested on  * macOS (local): R 4.4.x — 0 errors, 0 warnings, 1 note * win-builder (R-devel): 0 errors, 0 warnings, 1 note * win-builder (R-release): 0 errors, 0 warnings, 1 note  ## Note about the 1 NOTE  "New submission" — the package was previously on CRAN (v1.2, archived 2013) and this is a resubmission as v2.0.0 with major improvements.

title: Guia de Resubmissão ao CRAN — prLogistic v2.0.0
code: 1. devtools::document()           ← regenera NAMESPACE e man/ 2. devtools::check()              ← 0 errors, 0 warnings? 3. devtools::check_win_devel()    ← testa no Windows/R-devel 4. git add . && git push          ← sobe ao GitHub, dispara Actions 5. devtools::submit_cran()        ← envia ao CRAN 6. Aguardar e-mail do CRAN        ← 1–7 dias 7. Corrigir se necessário         ← incrementar versão, NEWS.md 8. git tag v2.0.0 && git push --tags

previous_headings: Example 1 — Low Birth Weight (LBW): clustered binary data
title: Reproducing the Examples from Amorim & Ospina (2021)
text: 244 mothers followed two pregnancies Salvador, Brazil. Outcome: low birth weight (< 2500 g). Clustering: two births per mother.
code:
data(LBW) cat("n obs =", nrow(LBW), "| mothers =", length(unique(LBW$ID)), "
") #> n obs = 488 | mothers = 188 cat("Prevalence of low birth weight:", round(mean(LBW$low == "Low"), 3), "

") #> Prevalence of low birth weight: 0.309 table(LBW$low, LBW$smoke) #>          #>           No Yes #>   Normal 223 114 #>   Low     70  81

previous_headings: Example 1 — Low Birth Weight (LBW): clustered binary data
title: Reproducing the Examples from Amorim & Ospina (2021)
code: LBW$low_bin   <- as.integer(LBW$low   == "Low") LBW$smoke_bin <- as.integer(LBW$smoke == "Yes") LBW$race_bin  <- as.integer(LBW$race  == "Non-white")  fit_lbw_glm <- glm(low_bin ~ smoke_bin + race_bin + age,                    family = binomial, data = LBW)  cat("--- Conditional PR (GLM) ---\n") #> --- Conditional PR (GLM) --- prLogisticDelta(fit_lbw_glm, standardisation = "conditional") #>  #> Prevalence Ratio Estimation via Logistic Regression #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>   Model        : glm  #>   Method       : delta  #>   Standardis.  : conditional  #>   Conf. level  : 95%  #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>  #>           Estimate   2.5%  97.5% #> smoke_bin   1.7437 1.3409 2.2674 #> race_bin    0.6017 0.3581 1.0111 #> age         1.4135 0.8644 2.3115 cat("--- Marginal PR (GLM) ---\n") #> --- Marginal PR (GLM) --- prLogisticDelta(fit_lbw_glm, standardisation = "marginal") #>  #> Prevalence Ratio Estimation via Logistic Regression #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>   Model        : glm  #>   Method       : delta  #>   Standardis.  : marginal  #>   Conf. level  : 95%  #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>  #>           Estimate   2.5%  97.5% #> smoke_bin   1.7467 1.3390 2.2786 #> race_bin    0.6397 0.3961 1.0330 #> age         1.3527 0.8664 2.1120

previous_headings: Example 1 — Low Birth Weight (LBW): clustered binary data
title: Reproducing the Examples from Amorim & Ospina (2021)
code: library(geepack) fit_lbw_gee <- geeglm(low_bin ~ smoke_bin + race_bin + age,                       family = binomial, id = ID,                       corstr = "exchangeable", data = LBW) cat("--- Marginal PR (GEE, exchangeable) ---\n") #> --- Marginal PR (GEE, exchangeable) --- prLogisticGEE(fit_lbw_gee) #>  #> Prevalence Ratio Estimation via Logistic Regression #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>   Model        : geeglm  #>   Method       : delta  #>   Standardis.  : marginal  #>   Conf. level  : 95%  #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>  #>           Estimate   2.5%  97.5% #> smoke_bin   1.5927 1.1019 2.3021 #> race_bin    0.6382 0.3424 1.1898 #> age         1.4748 1.0557 2.0604

--- Marginal PR ---
") #>  #> --- Marginal PR --- res_uis_marg <- prLogisticDelta(fit_uis, standardisation = "marginal") print(res_uis_marg) #>  #> Prevalence Ratio Estimation via Logistic Regression #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>   Model        : glm  #>   Method       : delta  #>   Standardis.  : marginal  #>   Conf. level  : 95%  #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>  #>             Estimate   2.5%  97.5% #> trtLong       1.3805 1.0321 1.8466 #> Age           0.6774 0.5002 0.9173 #> DrugUseLong   1.7197 1.2724 2.3242 #> raceOther     1.2309 0.9001 1.6834 #> siteB         1.1916 0.8822 1.6096

title: Reproducing the Examples from Amorim & Ospina (2021)
code: OR <- exp(coef(fit_uis)[-1]) PR_cond <- coef(res_uis_cond) PR_marg <- coef(res_uis_marg)  comp <- data.frame(   OR        = round(OR, 3),   PR_cond   = round(PR_cond, 3),   PR_marg   = round(PR_marg, 3) ) print(comp) #>                OR PR_cond PR_marg #> trtLong     1.569   1.423   1.381 #> Age         0.576   0.624   0.677 #> DrugUseLong 2.144   1.779   1.720 #> raceOther   1.345   1.267   1.231 #> siteB       1.284   1.222   1.192

title: Reproducing the Examples from Amorim & Ospina (2021)
text: table shows consistently overstates PR prevalence ~10%: prevalence increases, ratio /PR grows — confirming poor proxy PR common-outcome studies.
code: results <- data.frame(   Dataset    = c("LBW (GLM)", "Thailand (GLM)", "UIS", "downer", "Titanic"),   Prevalence = c(0.18, 0.16, 0.43, 0.50, 0.38),   Predictor  = c("smoke", "sex (Boy)", "trt (Long)", "Myopathy (Yes)", "sex (Male)"),   OR         = c(     round(exp(coef(fit_lbw_glm)["smoke_bin"]), 2),     round(exp(coef(fit_thai_glm)["sex_bin"]), 2),     round(exp(coef(fit_uis)["trtLong"]), 2),     round(exp(coef(fit_downer)["MyopathyYes"]), 2),     round(exp(coef(fit_titanic)["sexMale"]), 2)   ),   PR_cond = c(     round(coef(prLogisticDelta(fit_lbw_glm))["smoke_bin"], 2),     round(coef(prLogisticDelta(fit_thai_glm))["sex_bin"], 2),     round(coef(res_uis_cond)["trtLong"], 2),     round(coef(prLogisticDelta(fit_downer))["MyopathyYes"], 2),     round(coef(res_tit)["sexMale"], 2)   ) ) results$OR_over_PR <- round(results$OR / results$PR_cond, 2) print(results) #>                    Dataset Prevalence      Predictor    OR PR_cond OR_over_PR #> smoke_bin        LBW (GLM)       0.18          smoke  2.30    1.74       1.32 #> sex_bin     Thailand (GLM)       0.16      sex (Boy)  1.58    1.46       1.08 #> trtLong                UIS       0.43     trt (Long)  1.57    1.42       1.11 #> MyopathyYes         downer       0.50 Myopathy (Yes)  0.26    0.30       0.87 #> sexMale            Titanic       0.38     sex (Male) 12.15    4.86       2.50

title: Estimating Prevalence Ratios with prLogistic
text: cross-sectional epidemiological studies, prevalence ratio (PR) often measure association interest. logistic regression used adjust confounders, model directly yields odds ratios (), PRs. Although approximates PR outcome rare (< 10%), approximation breaks common outcomes can substantially overestimate strength association (Zhang Yu 1998). prLogistic package estimates adjusted PRs — confidence intervals — directly logistic regression models, using two standardisation procedures (Wilcosky Chambless 1985; Amorim Ospina 2021): Conditional standardisation: PR fixed covariate values (reference profile). Marginal standardisation: population-averaged PR observed covariate distribution. procedures support four model types:

title: Estimating Prevalence Ratios with prLogistic
code: # CRAN (stable) install.packages("prLogistic")  # Development version (GitHub) # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("Raydonal/prLogistic")

title: Estimating Prevalence Ratios with prLogistic
text: use birthwt dataset MASS package, retrospective study risk factors low birth weight (n = 189).
code: data(birthwt, package = "MASS")  # Recode predictors birthwt$smoke <- factor(birthwt$smoke, labels = c("Non-smoker", "Smoker")) birthwt$race  <- factor(birthwt$race,                         labels = c("White", "Black", "Other")) birthwt$ht    <- factor(birthwt$ht,   labels = c("No", "Yes")) birthwt$ui    <- factor(birthwt$ui,   labels = c("No", "Yes"))  # Outcome prevalence mean(birthwt$low)   # 31 % — common outcome, OR is a poor approximation #> [1] 0.3121693

title: Estimating Prevalence Ratios with prLogistic
code: fit_glm <- glm(low ~ smoke + race + age + lwt + ht + ui,                family = binomial, data = birthwt)

title: Estimating Prevalence Ratios with prLogistic
text: Reference profile: binary predictors 0 (reference category), continuous predictors (age, lwt) sample medians.
code: res_cond <- prLogisticDelta(fit_glm, standardisation = "conditional") res_cond #>  #> Prevalence Ratio Estimation via Logistic Regression #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>   Model        : glm  #>   Method       : delta  #>   Standardis.  : conditional  #>   Conf. level  : 95%  #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>  #>             Estimate   2.5%  97.5% #> smokeSmoker   2.2850 1.1962 4.3649 #> raceBlack     2.7207 1.2501 5.9211 #> raceOther     2.0850 1.0226 4.2513 #> age           0.9851 0.9343 1.0386 #> lwt           0.9919 0.9888 0.9951 #> htYes         3.8333 1.7309 8.4890 #> uiYes         2.0750 1.0469 4.1128

title: Estimating Prevalence Ratios with prLogistic
text: random effect (1 | race) accounts unobserved -group heterogeneity. Fixed-effect coefficients — hence PRs — conditional random effects.
code: library(lme4) #> Loading required package: Matrix  # Treat race as a clustering variable (illustrative) fit_glmer <- glmer(low ~ smoke + age + lwt + ht + (1 | race),                    family = binomial, data = birthwt)  prLogisticDelta(fit_glmer, standardisation = "marginal") #>  #> Prevalence Ratio Estimation via Logistic Regression #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>   Model        : glmer  #>   Method       : delta  #>   Standardis.  : marginal  #>   Conf. level  : 95%  #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>  #>             Estimate   2.5%  97.5% #> smokeSmoker   1.7531 1.0756 2.8572 #> age           0.9871 0.9599 1.0151 #> lwt           0.9965 0.9942 0.9988 #> htYes         2.2071 1.3152 3.7039

title: Estimating Prevalence Ratios with prLogistic
text: GEE models yield population-averaged estimates naturally accommodate within-subject correlation. prLogisticGEE() wrapper sets marginal default standardisation. robust sandwich variance vcov(fit_gee) used automatically, providing valid inference even working correlation structure misspecified.
code: library(geepack) data(ohio, package = "geepack")  # Respiratory symptoms in children (4 repeated measures per child) fit_gee <- geeglm(resp ~ smoke + age,                   family = binomial,                   id     = id,                   corstr = "exchangeable",                   data   = ohio)  prLogisticGEE(fit_gee) #>  #> Prevalence Ratio Estimation via Logistic Regression #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>   Model        : geeglm  #>   Method       : delta  #>   Standardis.  : marginal  #>   Conf. level  : 95%  #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>  #>       Estimate   2.5%  97.5% #> smoke   1.2499 0.9332 1.6742 #> age     0.9070 0.8410 0.9782

title: Estimating Prevalence Ratios with prLogistic
text: Design-consistent (sandwich) standard errors incorporated automatically vcov() method svyglm objects.
code: library(survey) #> Loading required package: grid #> Loading required package: survival #>  #> Attaching package: 'survey' #> The following object is masked from 'package:graphics': #>  #>     dotchart data(api, package = "survey")  apiclus2$target_met <- as.numeric(apiclus2$sch.wide == "Yes")  # Two-stage cluster sample dclus2 <- svydesign(id   = ~dnum + snum,                     fpc  = ~fpc1 + fpc2,                     data = apiclus2)  fit_svy <- svyglm(target_met ~ meals + stype,                   design = dclus2,                   family = quasibinomial)  prLogisticSurvey(fit_svy, standardisation = "conditional") #>  #> Prevalence Ratio Estimation via Logistic Regression #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>   Model        : svyglm  #>   Method       : delta  #>   Standardis.  : conditional  #>   Conf. level  : 95%  #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>  #>        Estimate   2.5%  97.5% #> meals    0.9996 0.9989 1.0003 #> stypeH   0.1491 0.0505 0.4403 #> stypeM   0.6616 0.4246 1.0310

text: odds ratio overestimates PR outcome common. table illustrates smoking predictor birthwt example: 31% baseline prevalence (2.79) substantially overstates PR (2.29).
code: OR  <- exp(coef(fit_glm)["smokeSmoker"]) PR  <- coef(res_cond)["smokeSmoker"]  data.frame(   Measure  = c("Odds Ratio (logistic)", "Prevalence Ratio (conditional)",                "Prevalence Ratio (marginal)"),   Estimate = round(c(OR, PR, coef(res_marg)["smokeSmoker"]), 3) ) #>                          Measure Estimate #> 1          Odds Ratio (logistic)    2.794 #> 2 Prevalence Ratio (conditional)    2.285 #> 3    Prevalence Ratio (marginal)    1.808

title: Estimating Prevalence Ratios with prLogistic
text: predictor XjX_j (binary), adjusted PR : PR̂j=expit⁡(β̂0+β̂j+∑k≠jβ̂krk)expit⁡(β̂0+∑k≠jβ̂krk) \widehat{PR}_j = \frac{\operatorname{expit}(\hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_j +       \sum_{k \neq j} \hat\beta_k r_k)}      {\operatorname{expit}(\hat\beta_0 +       \sum_{k \neq j} \hat\beta_k r_k)} rkr_k reference value covariate kk (0 binary/dummy predictors; sample median mean continuous predictors).

title: Estimating Prevalence Ratios with prLogistic
text: PR̂j=n−1∑iexpit⁡(η̂(1))n−1∑iexpit⁡(η̂(0)) \widehat{PR}_j = \frac{n^{-1}\sum_i \operatorname{expit}(\hat\eta_i^{(1)})}      {n^{-1}\sum_i \operatorname{expit}(\hat\eta_i^{(0)})} η̂(1)\hat\eta_i^{(1)} η̂(0)\hat\eta_i^{(0)} linear predictors XijX_{ij} set 1 0, respectively.

title: Estimating Prevalence Ratios with prLogistic
text: Confidence intervals use first-order Taylor (delta method) approximation (Oliveira et al. 1997): Var̂[log⁡(PR̂)]≈𝐱*′Σ̂𝐱* \widehat{\operatorname{Var}}[\log(\widehat{PR})] \approx \mathbf{x}^*{}' \hat\Sigma \, \mathbf{x}^* 𝐱*=(1−p̂1)𝐱1−(1−p̂0)𝐱0\mathbf{x}^* = (1-\hat p_1)\mathbf{x}_1 - (1-\hat p_0)\mathbf{x}_0 Σ̂\hat\Sigma estimated covariance matrix 𝛃̂\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}.

text: prLogistic estimates adjusted prevalence ratios (PR) confidence intervals logistic regression models, addressing well-known limitation odds ratios () approximations PR cross-sectional studies common binary outcomes.

title: prLogistic — Prevalence Ratios via Logistic Regression
text: outcome common (prevalence > 10%), logistic regression overestimates PR. example, 30% baseline prevalence = 2.5, true PR closer 1.8. prLogistic corrects using conditional marginal standardisation directly logistic model.

title: prLogistic — Prevalence Ratios via Logistic Regression
code: # Stable release from CRAN install.packages("prLogistic")  # Development version from GitHub # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("Raydonal/prLogistic")

title: prLogistic — Prevalence Ratios via Logistic Regression
code: library(prLogistic)  # Fit a standard logistic model data(birthwt, package = "MASS") birthwt$smoke <- factor(birthwt$smoke) birthwt$race  <- factor(birthwt$race)  fit <- glm(low ~ smoke + race + age + lwt,            family = binomial, data = birthwt)  # --- Delta method --------------------------------------------------------  # Conditional PR (default: continuous covariates at median) prLogisticDelta(fit, standardisation = "conditional")  # Marginal PR (population-averaged) prLogisticDelta(fit, standardisation = "marginal")  # Custom reference: age = 25, lwt = 55 kg prLogisticDelta(fit,                 standardisation = "conditional",                 ref_values = list(age = 25, lwt = 55))  # --- Bootstrap CIs ------------------------------------------------------- set.seed(42) prLogisticBootCond(fit, data = birthwt, R = 999)  # --- Forest plot --------------------------------------------------------- res <- prLogisticDelta(fit) plot(res)

what: prLogistic: Estimation of Prevalence Ratios via Logistic Regression Models — prLogistic-package
title: prLogistic: Estimation of Prevalence Ratios via Logistic Regression Models — prLogistic-package
text: Estimates adjusted prevalence ratios (PR) confidence intervals logistic regression models, addressing well-known limitation odds ratios () approximations PR cross-sectional studies common outcomes. Supports independent observations (glm()), clustered/multilevel data (glmer() 'lme4'), longitudinal data via Generalised Estimating Equations (geeglm() 'geepack'), complex survey designs (svyglm() 'survey'). Inference available via delta method (conditional marginal standardisation) via bootstrap (normal-approximation percentile intervals). Continuous covariates handled user-specified median-based reference values; flexible baseline specification allows reference category chosen factor predictors. Based methodology described Amorim & Ospina (2021) doi:10.1590/0001-3765202120190316 .

title: prLogistic: Estimation of Prevalence Ratios via Logistic Regression Models — prLogistic-package

title: Estimate Prevalence Ratios via Logistic Regression – Delta Method — prLogisticDelta
text: Conditional standardisation fixes covariates reference values (median/mean continuous, 0 binary/dummy) computes PR predictor contrasting exposed (predictor = 1) vs unexposed (predictor = 0) profiles: $$   \widehat{PR}_j =   \frac{\mathrm{expit}(\hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_j + \sum_{k \neq j} \hat\beta_k r_k)}        {\mathrm{expit}(\hat\beta_0 + \sum_{k \neq j} \hat\beta_k r_k)} $$ \(r_k\) reference values remaining covariates. Marginal standardisation computes counterfactual prevalences using observed covariate distribution entire sample: $$   \widehat{PR}_j =   \frac{n^{-1}\sum_i \mathrm{expit}(\hat\eta_i^{(1)})}        {n^{-1}\sum_i \mathrm{expit}(\hat\eta_i^{(0)})} $$ \(\hat\eta_i^{(1)}\) \(\hat\eta_i^{(0)}\) linear predictors predictor \(j\) set 1 0, respectively. Variance estimates use delta method (first-order Taylor expansion) described Oliveira et al. (1997) Amorim & Ospina (2021).

title: Estimate Prevalence Ratios via Logistic Regression – Delta Method — prLogisticDelta

F., Santana, V. S. & Lopes, . . (1997). Razoes de proporcoes e uso da regressao log?stica em estudos transversais. Revista de Sa?de P?blica, 31, 90-99. Wilcosky, T. C. & Chambless, L. E. (1985). comparison direct adjustment regression adjustment epidemiologic measures. Journal Chronic Diseases, 38, 849-856.

code: # --- Independent observations (glm) --- infert is a built-in dataset ---- # outcome: case (spontaneous abortion), prevalence ~33% fit_glm <- glm(case ~ induced + spontaneous + parity,                family = binomial, data = infert)  # Conditional PR (continuous covariates at median) prLogisticDelta(fit_glm, standardisation = "conditional") #>  #> Prevalence Ratio Estimation via Logistic Regression #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>   Model        : glm  #>   Method       : delta  #>   Standardis.  : conditional  #>   Conf. level  : 95%  #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>  #>             Estimate   2.5%  97.5% #> induced       2.5500 1.5221 4.2720 #> spontaneous   4.3606 2.5417 7.4814 #> parity        0.5941 0.4497 0.7850 #>   # Marginal PR prLogisticDelta(fit_glm, standardisation = "marginal") #>  #> Prevalence Ratio Estimation via Logistic Regression #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>   Model        : glm  #>   Method       : delta  #>   Standardis.  : marginal  #>   Conf. level  : 95%  #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>  #>             Estimate   2.5%  97.5% #> induced       1.7024 1.1632 2.4914 #> spontaneous   3.0923 2.0353 4.6983 #> parity        0.8005 0.7103 0.9021 #>   # Custom reference values prLogisticDelta(fit_glm,                 standardisation = "conditional",                 ref_values = list(parity = 2)) #>  #> Prevalence Ratio Estimation via Logistic Regression #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>   Model        : glm  #>   Method       : delta  #>   Standardis.  : conditional  #>   Conf. level  : 95%  #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>  #>             Estimate   2.5%  97.5% #> induced       2.5500 1.5221 4.2720 #> spontaneous   4.3606 2.5417 7.4814 #> parity        0.5941 0.4497 0.7850 #>   if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # --- Clustered data (glmer) --------------------------------------------- library(lme4) fit_glmer <- glmer(case ~ induced + spontaneous + (1 | stratum),                    family = binomial, data = infert) prLogisticDelta(fit_glmer, standardisation = "marginal")  # --- Longitudinal / GEE ------------------------------------------------- library(geepack) data(ohio, package = "geepack") fit_gee <- geeglm(resp ~ smoke + age,                   family  = binomial,                   id      = id,                   corstr  = "exchangeable",                   data    = ohio) prLogisticDelta(fit_gee, standardisation = "marginal")  # --- Complex survey design ---------------------------------------------- library(survey) data(api, package = "survey") dclus2 <- svydesign(id = ~dnum + snum, fpc = ~fpc1 + fpc2, data = apiclus2) fit_svy <- svyglm(sch.wide ~ meals + stype,                   design = dclus2, family = quasibinomial) prLogisticDelta(fit_svy, standardisation = "conditional") } # }

text: GEE accounts within-subject correlation working correlation structure (corstr argument geeglm()). Common choices: "independence" correlation assumed (equivalent GLM). "exchangeable" Constant correlation across time points. "ar1" First-order autoregressive; suitable ordered time. "unstructured" Estimates pairwise correlations freely. robust (sandwich) variance-covariance matrix returned vcov() geeglm object used automatically, giving valid inference even working correlation structure misspecified.

title: prLogistic 2.0.1 (2026-06-06)
text: CRAN resubmission. Single-quoted software names added parentheses function names DESCRIPTION, removed broken LICENSE link README, following CRAN feedback. changes functionality.

title: prLogistic 2.0.0 (2025-06-05)
text: Minimum R version raised 4.1.0. prLogisticDelta() now takes fitted model object first argument (instead formula + dataset). aligns standard R modelling conventions allows function handle supported model types. cluster pattern arguments old prLogisticDelta() replaced standardisation. Use standardisation = "conditional" (default) standardisation = "marginal". Hmisc dependency removed (unused). Return value now prLogistic S3 object rather plain matrix, print, summary, coef, confint, plot methods.

title: prLogistic 2.0.0 (2025-06-05)
text: Continuous covariates: reference value defaults sample median (configurable via ref_continuous = "mean"). old implementation supported binary (0/1) predictors. Flexible baseline: use ref_values = list(var = value) set reference value predictor. overrides automatic defaults model’s contrast coding. GEE support (prLogisticGEE()): prevalence ratios longitudinal / repeated-measures data fitted geepack::geeglm(). Robust (sandwich) variance used automatically. Complex survey support (prLogisticSurvey()): prevalence ratios data complex survey designs fitted survey::svyglm(). Design-consistent variance used automatically. plot.prLogistic(): base-R forest plot quick visualisation PR estimates confidence intervals. Improved delta-method gradient marginal standardisation: uses full sample-averaged counterfactual gradient (single fixed row), giving accurate variance estimates.

title: prLogistic 2.0.0 (2025-06-05)
text: Fixed incorrect variance calculation pr_marginal() models one continuous covariate. Negative confidence interval bounds longer silently truncated 0 without warning; diagnostic warning now issued. pr.conditional() longer crashes glmerMod objects use lme4::fixef() instead @beta (slot accessor fragile across lme4 versions).

title: prLogistic 2.0.0 (2025-06-05)
text: Complete rewrite using roxygen2 documentation. internal functions prefixed . avoid namespace pollution. Comprehensive testthat (edition 3) test suite covering utilities, delta method, bootstrap, GEE, survey model types. pkgdown website _pkgdown.yml configuration. GitHub Actions CI/CD workflow multi-platform R CMD check automatic pkgdown deployment.
